_comment: Editorial calendar for Protein of the Week. Three levels: season (quarter) -> collection (month) -> specimen (week). research.py flattens the specimens in order (week number = position), picks the first one with no issue JSON yet (and status != 'published'), and passes its collection + season theme to the researchers and writer as light context. 'axis' names what ties a group together (family, function, provenance, impact, discovery, disease, or any non-obvious connection); it is editorial metadata and can differ per level. 'blurb' is the reader-facing announcement line. 'anchor_date' is week 1's publish date (a Monday); a specimen's reveal week is anchor_date + 7*(position-1) days, used for teasers. Add or reorder freely; numbering follows position. The 'bench' array holds unscheduled seasons as swap inventory: nothing in the harness or renderer walks it, so week numbers and the catalogue are unaffected. To schedule a bench season, move its object into 'seasons' at the desired position (it then picks up week numbers and reveal dates automatically) and give its season/collections real quarter/month ids; to shelve one, move it back.

seasons.label: The Instruments
seasons.blurb: Proteins that stopped being something biologists studied and became something they use.

collections.label: Seeing
collections.blurb: Proteins that made the invisible visible.

specimens.hint: Photinus pyralis bioluminescence; ATP-dependent light from luciferin; ubiquitous reporter gene and ATP assay.
E. coli lac operon (Jacob & Monod); blue-white screening with X-gal; a founding reporter enzyme.
DsRed from Discosoma coral; Tsien lab engineered the monomeric 'mFruit' palette (mCherry, mOrange), extending fluorescent tags beyond green.

collections.label: Copying
collections.blurb: The enzymes that let us read, amplify, and rewrite the code.

specimens.hint: From Thermus aquaticus, a Yellowstone hot-spring bacterium (Brock, 1969); thermostable; made PCR practical; Kary Mullis, 1993 Nobel.
RNA-dependent DNA polymerase; Temin & Baltimore, 1970 (Nobel 1975); retroviruses; the basis of cDNA, RT-PCR, and RNA-seq.
Single-subunit polymerase from bacteriophage T7; in vitro transcription and the pET expression systems.
From bacteriophage T4; seals nicks and joins fragments; the workhorse of molecular cloning.

collections.label: Cutting & editing
collections.blurb: Proteins that perform surgery on DNA and on other proteins.

specimens.hint: E. coli restriction endonuclease; cuts the palindrome GAATTC; central to the Cohen-Boyer recombinant-DNA revolution.
CRISPR-associated nuclease from Streptococcus pyogenes; Doudna & Charpentier, 2012; 2020 Nobel; guided genome editing.
From bacteriophage P1; site-specific recombination at loxP sites; conditional and tissue-specific gene knockouts.
Tobacco Etch Virus NIa protease; highly specific ENLYFQ/S cleavage; the molecular scalpel used to remove affinity tags.

seasons.label: Nature's Pharmacy
seasons.blurb: Molecules that evolved to harm, redeployed to heal.

collections.label: Venom to vial
collections.blurb: Animal venoms that became medicines.

specimens.hint: Synthetic exendin-4 from Gila monster (Heloderma suspectum) saliva; GLP-1 receptor agonist for type 2 diabetes (Byetta).
Synthetic omega-conotoxin MVIIA from the cone snail Conus magus; N-type calcium channel blocker for severe chronic pain (Prialt).
Bradykinin-potentiating peptide from the pit viper Bothrops jararaca; teprotide inspired captopril, the first ACE inhibitor.
Derived from barbourin, a disintegrin in pygmy rattlesnake (Sistrurus miliarius barbouri) venom; GPIIb/IIIa antiplatelet drug.

collections.label: The toxin toolbox
collections.blurb: Bacterial toxins turned into tools and cures.

specimens.hint: From Clostridium botulinum; cleaves SNAREs to block acetylcholine release; the deadliest known toxin, and Botox for dystonia and cosmetics.
From Corynebacterium diphtheriae; ADP-ribosylates EF-2; the basis of immunotoxins and, as a toxoid, the DTP vaccine.
From Vibrio cholerae; ADP-ribosylates Gs-alpha; a classic G-protein signaling probe; the B subunit is an adjuvant and neural tracer.
From Clostridium tetani; cleaves synaptobrevin; a retrograde axonal-transport tracer, and as a toxoid the tetanus vaccine.

collections.label: Hormones we learned to make
collections.blurb: The body's own proteins, turned into medicine.

specimens.hint: Banting & Best (Toronto, 1921); Sanger's sequencing (1951, first protein sequenced); first recombinant-DNA drug, Humulin (Genentech / Eli Lilly, 1982).
Somatotropin; once pituitary-derived (until CJD risk), then recombinant (Protropin, 1985); treats growth disorders.
EPO; drives red-cell production; recombinant (Epogen, Amgen, 1989) for anemia; infamous as a doping agent.
Opposing pancreatic and gut hormones of glucose control; GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide) now treat diabetes and obesity.

seasons.label: The Immune Arsenal
seasons.blurb: Antibodies and the biologics that grew from them.

collections.label: The antibody
collections.blurb: The Y-shaped molecule and the first drugs made from it.

specimens.hint: The Y-shaped antibody; Edelman & Porter worked out its structure (Nobel 1972); the workhorse of adaptive immunity.
Kohler & Milstein's hybridoma (1975, Nobel 1984); one antibody against one target; the birth of antibody therapeutics.
Anti-CD20 chimeric monoclonal; the first monoclonal antibody approved for cancer (1997); B-cell lymphoma.
Herceptin; humanized anti-HER2 monoclonal (Slamon); targeted therapy for HER2-positive breast cancer.

collections.label: Checkpoints & signals
collections.blurb: Proteins that turn the immune system up and down.

specimens.hint: Honjo's checkpoint; blockade (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) unleashes T cells on tumors; 2018 Nobel.
Allison's checkpoint; ipilimumab was the first checkpoint inhibitor approved; 2018 Nobel.
Inflammatory cytokine; blockers (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept) transformed rheumatoid arthritis and IBD.
Isaacs & Lindenmann, 1957; antiviral cytokines; IFN-alpha for hepatitis, IFN-beta for multiple sclerosis.

collections.label: Engineered immunity
collections.blurb: Immune proteins redesigned by human hands.

specimens.hint: Chimeric antigen receptor; T cells re-armed against cancer; tisagenlecleucel (Kymriah) for leukemia.
Blinatumomab (CD19 x CD3 BiTE) grabs a T cell with one arm and a tumor cell with the other.
Camelid single-domain VHH (Hamers-Casterman, 1993); tiny and stable; caplacizumab and countless research reagents.
Etanercept (a TNF receptor fused to antibody Fc); receptor 'traps' like aflibercept; a whole class of biologics.

seasons.label: The Machinery of the Cell
seasons.blurb: The fundamental molecular machines that keep a cell alive.

collections.label: Motors
collections.blurb: Proteins that move.

specimens.hint: Actin-based motor of muscle; Huxley's sliding-filament model; a vast superfamily beyond muscle.
Microtubule plus-end motor; anterograde axonal transport; discovered by Vale & Sheetz, 1985.
Microtubule minus-end motor; retrograde transport and the beating of cilia and flagella.
A rotary motor that makes ATP; Boyer & Walker's binding-change mechanism (Nobel 1997); Mitchell's chemiosmosis.

collections.label: Scaffolds & shape
collections.blurb: Proteins that build structure.

specimens.hint: The most abundant cytoskeletal protein; microfilaments; cell shape, division, and motility.
Alpha/beta subunit of microtubules; the mitotic spindle; the target of taxol and colchicine.
The most abundant protein in mammals; a triple helix; connective tissue; the vitamin-C link to scurvy.
The largest known protein (~34,000 residues); the molecular spring of the sarcomere; muscle elasticity.

collections.label: Information machines
collections.blurb: Proteins that read and write the code of life.

specimens.hint: Transcribes DNA into RNA; Roger Kornberg's structures (Nobel 2006); a multisubunit machine.
A ribonucleoprotein machine that translates mRNA into protein; Ramakrishnan, Steitz, Yonath (Nobel 2009).
Replicates DNA; Arthur Kornberg's Pol I (Nobel 1959); proofreading and replication fidelity.
Spool DNA into nucleosomes; the histone code; the foundation of chromatin and epigenetics.

seasons.label: The Molecular Diseases
seasons.blurb: Proteins defined by what goes wrong when they break; Pauling called sickle-cell the first molecular disease.

collections.label: One letter's difference
collections.blurb: Single mutations with outsized consequences.

specimens.hint: Oxygen carrier; Pauling's 'molecular disease' (1949) and Ingram's single amino-acid substitution (1957) explained sickle-cell anemia.
Cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator; the F508del mutation; gene cloned in 1989 by Tsui, Riordan, and Collins.
Duchenne muscular dystrophy; product of the largest human gene; identified by Kunkel, 1987.
Phenylketonuria (PKU); Folling, 1934; the basis of the Guthrie newborn heel-prick screen.

collections.label: The shape of forgetting
collections.blurb: Proteins that misfold and clump, and the diseases that follow.

specimens.hint: Stanley Prusiner's protein-only infectious agent (Nobel 1997); scrapie, CJD, and mad cow disease.
The plaque-forming peptide of Alzheimer's disease; cleaved from APP; characterized by Glenner and Wong, 1984.
The main component of Parkinson's Lewy bodies; linked to disease by Goedert and Spillantini, 1997.
Microtubule-associated protein whose tangles define Alzheimer's and the tauopathies.

collections.label: When the blood won't stop
collections.blurb: The clotting cascade, seen through the diseases of its failure.

specimens.hint: Hemophilia A, the 'royal disease'; the first recombinant clotting factor, 1992.
Platelet-adhesion protein; its deficiency is the most common inherited bleeding disorder.
Thrombin cleaves it into fibrin, the mesh of a blood clot.
The cascade's brake; heparin works through it; its deficiency causes thrombosis.

seasons.label: First Light: the founding enzymes
seasons.blurb: The enzymes that built biochemistry, from the first one named to the first ones seen.

collections.label: Named before they were known
collections.blurb: The enzymes that gave enzymology its start, and its vocabulary.

specimens.hint: Payen and Persoz, 1833: the first enzyme described; its name is the root of the '-ase' suffix.
Theodor Schwann named the stomach's digestive principle in 1836; later crystallized by Northrop (Nobel 1946).
Wilhelm Kuhne characterized it in 1876 and, in 1877, coined the word 'enzyme.'
James Sumner crystallized it in 1926, the first proof that enzymes are proteins (Nobel 1946).

collections.label: The first structures
collections.blurb: The enzymes whose atomic shapes we saw first.

specimens.hint: Discovered by Fleming in 1922; in 1965, the first enzyme structure and mechanism, solved by David Phillips.
Anfinsen's refolding showed sequence dictates structure (Nobel 1972); Merrifield's total synthesis (Nobel 1984).
The serine-protease catalytic triad; an early window into how enzymes actually work.
William Lipscomb's structure; an early view of a zinc metalloenzyme's active site.

collections.label: Catalytic perfection
collections.blurb: Enzymes that run up against the physical limits of speed.

specimens.hint: Near the diffusion limit; interconverts carbon dioxide and bicarbonate millions of times a second.
The textbook 'catalytically perfect' enzyme, limited only by how fast substrate arrives.
Clears the neurotransmitter at the synapse at near-diffusion-limited rates; the target of nerve agents.
Destroys penicillins almost as fast as they arrive; a chief engine of antibiotic resistance.

seasons.label: The Oxygen Chronicles
seasons.blurb: Life's fraught relationship with oxygen: carrying it, burning it, and surviving it.

collections.label: Carrying it
collections.blurb: The famous oxygen carrier's lesser-known cousins, and how other lineages move oxygen.

specimens.hint: Kendrew's 1958 structure was the first of any protein (Nobel 1962); stores oxygen in muscle.
Copper-based oxygen carrier; the blue blood of snails, octopuses, and horseshoe crabs.
A plant globin in legume root nodules; shields nitrogen-fixing enzymes from oxygen.
A non-heme, iron-based oxygen carrier found in a few marine invertebrate phyla.

collections.label: Burning it
collections.blurb: The electron-transport chain, where oxygen meets its end.

specimens.hint: A mobile electron carrier so conserved it serves as a molecular evolutionary clock; also triggers apoptosis.
Complex IV; the terminal step that hands electrons to oxygen to make water.
An ancient iron-sulfur electron carrier at the heart of metabolism.
Complex I; the largest and first enzyme of the respiratory chain.

collections.label: Making it, taming it
collections.blurb: The protein that releases oxygen, and the ones that defend against its damage.

specimens.hint: Splits water and releases the oxygen we breathe; its manganese cluster is the only known biological water-oxidizer.
The most abundant protein on Earth; fixes atmospheric carbon, famously slowly.
Neutralizes the superoxide radical, a toxic byproduct of oxygen metabolism.
Breaks hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen at one of the highest turnover rates known.

seasons.label: Signals & Switches
seasons.blurb: How a cell senses its world and decides what to do about it.

collections.label: The receptors
collections.blurb: The antennae that read the outside world.

specimens.hint: The light receptor of the eye; the first G-protein-coupled receptor structure (Palczewski, 2000).
The model GPCR; Lefkowitz and Kobilka's work on it won the 2012 Nobel.
The first neurotransmitter receptor isolated, from the electric ray's electric organ.
A receptor tyrosine kinase; the cell-surface half of the insulin story.

collections.label: The switches
collections.blurb: The molecular toggles that relay and amplify a signal.

specimens.hint: A GTPase that switches growth signaling on and off; mutated in roughly a quarter of human cancers.
The prototype protein kinase; Krebs and Fischer's phosphorylation work (Nobel 1992).
A small, ubiquitous calcium sensor that relays the calcium signal to dozens of targets.
The 'guardian of the genome'; the most frequently mutated tumor-suppressor protein.

collections.label: Gates & channels
collections.blurb: Proteins that make cells electrical.

specimens.hint: MacKinnon's structure revealed the selectivity filter (Nobel 2003).
Carries the action potential; the molecular basis of Hodgkin and Huxley's nerve impulse.
The water channel; Peter Agre's discovery shared the 2003 Nobel.
The mechanosensitive channel behind touch and pressure sensing; Patapoutian's 2021 Nobel.

bench.label: The Green World
bench.blurb: Plant proteins: catching light, fighting back, and ripening.

collections.label: Catching light
collections.blurb: How plants harvest and read light.

specimens.hint: The second photosystem; passes light-excited electrons on to make NADPH.
The chlorophyll-and-carotenoid antenna that funnels photons to the reaction center.
A red / far-red light switch that tells a plant about day length and shade.
A blue-light sensor tied to plant growth and to animal circadian clocks and magnetoreception.

collections.label: The arsenal
collections.blurb: The proteins plants use to defend themselves.

specimens.hint: A ribosome-inactivating toxin from castor beans; infamous from the 1978 umbrella-tip assassination of Georgi Markov.
A jack-bean lectin that binds sugars; a workhorse reagent in glycobiology.
A wound-signaling peptide that triggers a tomato plant's defenses.
An intensely sweet protein from a West African fruit, thousands of times sweeter than sugar.

collections.label: Ripening & growth
collections.blurb: The proteins that soften, sweeten, and grow.

specimens.hint: The committed step in making ethylene, the gas hormone that ripens fruit.
Loosens the cell wall so a plant cell can grow.
A cysteine protease from papaya; a meat tenderizer and a classic mechanistic enzyme.
A pineapple protease used in food and folk medicine.

bench.label: Living Materials
bench.blurb: The structural proteins humans borrow for fibers, glues, and optics.

collections.label: Threads
collections.blurb: Protein fibers stronger than their weight suggests.

specimens.hint: Dragline spidroin; tougher for its weight than steel or Kevlar.
The silkworm's fiber; the material of the Silk Road and, now, of biomedical scaffolds.
The protein of hair, horn, wool, and feathers.
The recoil in skin, lungs, and arteries; it snaps back a billion times over a lifetime.

collections.label: Glues & springs
collections.blurb: Adhesives and elastics evolution reached first.

specimens.hint: A DOPA-based adhesive that sets underwater; the model for wet glues.
A nearly perfect elastic protein; it powers the flea's jump and the insect wing-hinge.
The tough anchoring threads a mussel spins to hold onto rock.
One of nature's most durable permanent adhesives.

collections.label: Optics & light
collections.blurb: Proteins that bend, store, or make light.

specimens.hint: Packed into the eye's lens at high concentration to stay transparent for a lifetime.
The tunable, iridescent protein behind squid and cuttlefish camouflage.
Binds tiny ice crystals to keep polar fish and insects from freezing.
The other Aequorea victoria protein: a calcium-triggered blue light that led Shimomura to GFP.
